Joseph's story takes a sharp turn in this chapter, and we need to feel the weight of what actually happens to him. He's sold into slavery by his own brothers, bought by Potiphar, and begins to prosper—so much that his master puts everything under his charge. The text is clear: "the LORD was with Joseph, and he became a successful man." This is not luck or cunning. This is the presence of God making Joseph fruitful even in captivity. But then Potiphar's wife desires him, and Joseph faces the kind of temptation that doesn't announce itself as obviously evil. She's not threatening him. She's seducing him. She's persistent. And she's his master's wife—which means refusing her could cost him everything he's built. Joseph chooses to flee instead.

What's striking is Joseph's reasoning when he first refuses her: "No one in this house is greater than I am. He has withheld nothing from me except you, because you are his wife. So how could I do such a great evil and sin against God?" Joseph doesn't say, "I might get caught," or "It would hurt my reputation." He calls it what it is—a sin against God. He recognizes that his loyalty to Potiphar and his trust in God are inseparable. This is the perspective of someone who understands that God sees what happens in darkness, that His claim on our obedience doesn't weaken just because no one else would know. When the moment comes and she grabs him, Joseph doesn't negotiate or hesitate. He runs, leaving his cloak behind rather than stay. He chooses shame and loss over sin.

But his obedience doesn't deliver him from suffering—it delivers him into it. Potiphar's wife lies, and Potiphar believes her. Joseph goes from trusted steward to prisoner. The injustice is staggering. He did the right thing and was punished for it. Yet the refrain repeats: "the LORD was with him and extended kindness to him." Even in prison, Joseph rises. Even in confinement, God grants him favor and puts him in charge. The chapter doesn't offer an explanation for why this happened to him or when it will end. It simply insists that God's presence and blessing don't depend on Joseph's circumstances. They don't vanish when he's falsely accused. They don't disappear behind prison walls.

This is what it costs to belong to God in a world hostile to His ways. It's not comfortable piety. It's the willingness to lose everything rather than betray your conscience before Him. And it's the faith to trust that His presence with you doesn't mean you'll be spared suffering—it means you won't be abandoned in it. Joseph doesn't know how his story ends when he's thrown into that prison. He only knows that God is with him. For now, that has to be enough.
